Module 1
Fundamentals of Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence

A

AI → ML → DL → Generative AI hierarchy

Latency, throughput, token pricing, custom costs
Prerequisites
None
Leads To
AWS AI services, deployment, cost optimization
Module Introduction

This module establishes the foundational vocabulary for all of AWS AI. It maps the AI hierarchy, explains how machines learn from data, and shows how AWS organizes its services around these concepts. Every term introduced here recurs throughout the MasterClass.

A
The AI Ecosystem
A
A1The Four Terms and Their Hierarchy
Artificial Intelligence
Umbrella field — enabling machines to simulate human intelligence
Machine Learning
Subset of AI — systems learn patterns from data, no explicit rules
Deep Learning
Subset of ML — uses layered neural networks for complex tasks
Generative AI
Subset of DL — creates new content (text, images, audio, code)

Nesting: AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL ⊃ Generative AI. Each is a proper subset of the one above — not alternatives.

Generative AI creates · Traditional ML analyzes or predicts
All Generative AI is DL, but not all DL is generative This nesting is the #1 exam diagram to memorize
B
Machine Learning Fundamentals
B
B1The ML Pipeline

Five-stage iterative cycle: Data Collection → Data Preparation (clean, preprocess) → Training (learn patterns) → Evaluation (measure performance) → Iteration (improve and repeat).

Model quality = Data quality · "Garbage in, garbage out"
Training happens periodically — inference happens continuously at scale
B2Data Types
Labeled
Data with correct answers attached. Required for supervised learning. Expensive at scale.
Unlabeled
Raw data, no annotations. Used in unsupervised and self-supervised learning. Abundant.
Structured
Predefined schema — tabular (rows/columns) or time-series (sequential timestamps).
Unstructured
No fixed format — text, images, video, audio. Majority of real-world data.
Labeled vs. Unlabeled → determines learning type Structured vs. Unstructured → determines algorithm and preprocessing
B3Learning Types
Supervised
Learns from labeled data — predicts outputs for unseen inputs (classification, regression)
Unsupervised
Finds hidden structure in unlabeled data — clustering, anomaly detection, dimensionality reduction
Reinforcement
Agent learns via reward/penalty signals from an environment — used in AWS DeepRacer
Semi-Supervised
Small labeled set + large unlabeled set. [+] Practical when labeling is costly.
B4Inference Modes
Batch Inference
Large dataset, processed at once. Not time-sensitive. Lower cost per prediction. Example: overnight scoring jobs.
Real-Time Inference
Instant, per-request predictions. Low latency required. Higher cost. Example: live recommendations.
C
Deep Learning and Neural Networks
C
C1Neural Network Architecture

Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) are layered computational structures of nodes (neurons) connected by weighted links. Learning = adjusting weights to minimize prediction error across training examples.

Input Layer
Receives raw data — one node per input feature
Hidden Layers
Learn progressively abstract representations. "Deep" = many hidden layers.
Output Layer
Produces final result — class label, probability, generated token
"Deep" refers to the number of hidden layers, not depth of understanding Backpropagation adjusts weights throughout the network after each prediction error [+]
C2Key Application Domains
Computer Vision
Image Classification · Object Detection · Segmentation
AWS: Amazon Rekognition
Natural Language Processing
Text Classification · Sentiment Analysis · Translation · Text Generation
AWS: Comprehend, Translate, Lex
D
Generative AI and Foundation Models
D
D1Foundation Models (FMs)

Foundation Models are large models pretrained on internet-scale, unlabeled data using self-supervised learning. They are general-purpose — a single FM can perform many tasks — and serve as a starting point that developers adapt for specific use cases instead of training from scratch.

Amazon Bedrock provides managed access to FMs from Anthropic, Meta, Mistral, and Amazon through a single API — no infrastructure management required.
FMs use self-supervised learning — no human labels required for pretraining Pretrained on internet-scale data → adaptable across domains
D2FM Lifecycle

Six stages: Data Selection (large unlabeled datasets) → Pre-training (self-supervised; Continuous Pre-Training expands domain knowledge) → Optimization (fine-tuning, RLHF) → Evaluation (benchmarks and metrics) → Deployment (production integration) → Feedback Loop (continuous improvement).

E
Generative Model Architectures
E
E1Large Language Models (LLMs)

Text-generating models built on the Transformer architecture. They process language through three key mechanisms:

Tokens
Smallest processing unit — words, subwords, characters, spaces. Standardizes input.
Embeddings
Numerical vector representations of tokens. Semantically similar words → vectors that are close together.
Vectors
Mathematical objects capturing semantic relationships. Enable the model to "understand" meaning.
Text → Tokens → Embeddings → Transformer layers → generate next token → repeat [+] "Cat," "feline," "kitten" → embeddings close in vector space
E2Diffusion Models

Architecture for image and audio generation. Two phases: Forward Diffusion (training) — gradually add noise to clean data until it becomes pure noise; Reverse Diffusion (inference) — iteratively remove noise from randomness until a coherent output emerges, guided by a prompt or condition.

Forward = add noise (training) · Reverse = remove noise (generation) Leading architecture for image generation (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E)
E3Multimodal Models

Process and generate across multiple data types simultaneously — text + image, image + audio. Used for visual question answering, video captioning, and generating graphics from text instructions.

E4Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)

Two competing networks: Generator (creates synthetic/fake data) vs. Discriminator (classifies real vs. fake). Training converges when the Generator produces fakes indistinguishable from real data. Largely superseded by Diffusion models for image generation due to training instability. [+]

Generator fools Discriminator · Discriminator trains Generator · Both improve together
E5Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)

Encoder compresses input to a lower-dimensional probability distribution (typically Gaussian). Decoder reconstructs or generates data from sampled points in that distribution. The probabilistic latent space enables controlled, predictable generation — you sample from a known distribution rather than fixed points. [+]

Encoder → distribution parameters (mean, variance) · Decoder → reconstruct from sample Gaussian distribution → predictable, controllable generation
F
Model Optimization Techniques
F
F1Prompt Engineering

Craft better inputs to get better outputs — no model changes. Four components: Instruction (define the task) · Context (background to guide interpretation) · Input Data (raw content to act on) · Output Indicator (specify format or constraints).

No weight modification · Fastest and cheapest optimization technique
F2Fine-Tuning and RLHF

Fine-tuning continues training a pretrained FM on a smaller, domain-specific dataset — modifies model weights to specialize behavior. Two variants: Instruction Tuning (train on prompt-response pairs) and RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback — humans rate responses; ratings train a reward model; FM is optimized to maximize reward).

Fine-tuning changes weights · More expensive than prompting · Produces deeper behavioral change
F3RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)

RAG retrieves relevant external documents at inference time and injects them into the prompt, giving the model access to current or private knowledge it was never trained on. Does not modify model weights — operates entirely at inference time.

RAG ≠ Fine-Tuning · RAG changes inputs, not weights
Use RAG for: private data, up-to-date info, reducing hallucination via grounding Cost hierarchy: Prompt Engineering (cheapest) → RAG → Fine-Tuning (most expensive)
G
AWS AI/ML Infrastructure
G
G1The Three-Layer Stack
ML Frameworks
Build, train, and deploy custom models — Amazon SageMaker
AI/ML Services
Pre-built task-specific APIs — no ML expertise required
Generative AI
Foundation Model access and AI assistants — Bedrock, Amazon Q
G2AI/ML Services — Know These
Comprehend
NLP insights — entities, sentiment, key phrases, topics
Translate
Neural machine translation, 75+ language pairs
Textract
OCR — extract text, tables, forms from documents
Lex
Chatbots/voice — ASR + NLU (understands commands and intent)
Polly
Text-to-speech (TTS) — voiceovers and accessibility
Transcribe
Speech-to-text only (ASR) — audio → text transcription
Rekognition
Image/video analysis — objects, faces, content moderation
Kendra
Intelligent enterprise search — natural language against internal docs
Personalize
Real-time personalized recommendations
DeepRacer
Reinforcement learning experimentation platform
Key distinction: Lex = chatbot (ASR + NLU, understands intent) · Transcribe = speech-to-text only · Polly = text-to-speech · Textract = documents/forms
G3Generative AI Layer
Amazon Bedrock
Managed FM access — Anthropic, Meta, Mistral, Amazon via one API
SageMaker JumpStart
Prebuilt ML solutions and one-click deployable models
Amazon Q
AI business assistant — answers questions from internal data
Amazon Q Developer
AI code generation, review, and debugging for developers
H
Advantages of AWS AI Solutions
H
H1Why AWS for AI
Faster Development
Prebuilt APIs ship AI features in days, not months
Scalability
Global infrastructure scales inference automatically
Cost Efficiency
Pay-as-you-go — no upfront GPU cluster investment
Flexibility
Access open-source and proprietary FMs through Bedrock and SageMaker
Integration
Native connectivity to S3, Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB
Security
Enterprise-grade encryption, IAM, VPC isolation, compliance certifications
AWS = rapid AI deployment without heavy infrastructure — key exam framing
I
Cost Considerations
I
I1Cost-Performance Trade-offs

Every performance dimension has a cost trade-off: lower latency costs more compute · higher availability costs more redundancy · larger models cost more per inference · custom models cost more to train and host.

Token-based Pricing
Pay per input/output token. Scales directly with usage. Default Bedrock FM billing.
Provisioned Throughput
Reserve dedicated capacity upfront. Higher fixed cost, lower per-request cost at high volume.
Custom Models
Highest cost — training compute + dedicated hosting for fine-tuned or proprietary models.
Optimal architecture balances cost, performance, and scalability — not just one dimension Token pricing = pay-per-use · Provisioned = consistent high-volume workloads
Module Summary
  • AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL ⊃ Generative AI — nested hierarchy. Generative AI creates; traditional ML analyzes or predicts.
  • ML quality = data quality. Pipeline: Collect → Prepare → Train → Evaluate → Iterate. Inference = prediction in production (batch vs. real-time).
  • Neural networks: input → hidden → output layers, learning via weight adjustment. FMs use self-supervised learning on internet-scale unlabeled data.
  • Optimization ladder: Prompt Engineering (no weight change) → RAG (external knowledge at inference, no weight change) → Fine-Tuning (modifies weights). RAG ≠ Fine-Tuning.
  • AWS AI stack: SageMaker (custom ML) · Managed AI APIs (10 task-specific services) · Generative AI layer (Bedrock, Q, JumpStart). Know which service maps to which task.
AWS-AI MasterClass · Module 01 · Fundamentals of Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence
